en.extract:
The yellow-lored tanager (Chlorothraupis frenata), also known as the olive tanager, is a species of bird in the cardinal family Cardinalidae that is found in South America along the eastern foothills of the Andes from southern Colombia to western Bolivia. It was formerly considered to be conspecific with Carmiol's tanager of Central America.


Taxonomy
The yellow-lored tanager was formally described in 1907 by the German ornithologist Hans von Berlepsch from a specimen collected in the Marcapata District of eastern Peru. He considered his specimen as a subspecies of Carmiol's tanager and coined the trinomial name Chlorothraupis carmioli frenata. The epithet frenata is from Latin frenatus meaning "bridled". The yellow-lored tanager is now treated as a separate species. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
The yellow-lored tanager is about 17 cm (6.7 in) in overall length. The upper parts are a dull olive green and the underparts are a rather paler olive green. The throat is slightly yellower than the rest of the underparts, and is streaked in the male while being a uniform pale yellow in the female. The underparts of the female are paler than those of the male, and the female has a yellowish patch in front of the eyes. The beak is relatively stout and is black in both sexes.


Behaviour
The yellow-lored tanager sometimes forms small groups of 3-4 individuals. It eats arthropods and fruit.


Status
The population of this bird has not been quantified but the total number of birds is thought to be declining. However, the bird has a very large range and the International Union for Conservation of Nature considers its conservation status to be of  "least concern".


References

The '''yellow-lored tanager''' ('''''Chlorothraupis frenata'''''), also known as the '''olive tanager''', is a species of bird in the cardinal [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Cardinalidae]] that is found in South America along the eastern foothills of the Andes from southern Colombia to western Bolivia. It was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with [[Carmiol's tanager]] of [[Central America]].

==Taxonomy==
The yellow-lored tanager was [[species description|formally described]] in 1907 by the German ornithologist [[Hans von Berlepsch]] from a specimen collected in the [[Marcapata District]] of eastern Peru. He considered his specimen as a [[subspecies]] of [[Carmiol's tanager]] and coined the [[trinomial name]] ''Chlorothraupis carmioli frenata''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Berlepsch  | first=Hans von | author-link=Hans von Berlepsch | date=1907 | chapter=Descriptions of new species and conspecies of Neotropical birds | editor-last=Sharpe | editor-first=R. Bowdler | editor-link=Richard Bowdler Sharpe | title=Proceedings of the Fourth International Ornithological Conference. London June 1905 | location=London | publisher=Delau and Co. | pages=347-371 [349-350] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/34039571 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1970 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=13 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=282 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14483517 }}</ref> The epithet ''frenata'' is from [[Latin]] ''frenatus'' meaning "bridled".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n164/mode/1up }}</ref> The yellow-lored tanager is now treated as a separate species.<ref>{{ cite web | Classification Committee, American Ornithologists' Union | url=http://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCprop950.htm | access-date=14 July 2023}}</ref><ref>{{ cite web | last=Remsen | first=Van | date=May url=http://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCprop972.htm | access-date=14 July 2023}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Chesser | first1=R.T. | last2=Billerman | first2=S.M. | last3=Burns | first3=K.J. | journal=Ornithology | volume=140 | issue= | pages=1-11 | doi=10.1093/ornithology/ukad023 | doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=July 2023 | title=Cardinals, grosbeaks and (tanager) allies | work=IOC World Bird List Version 13.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/cardinals/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=14 July 2023 }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc/>

==Description==
The yellow-lored tanager is about {{cvt|17|cm}} in overall length. The upper parts are a dull olive green and the underparts are a rather paler olive green. The throat is slightly yellower than the rest of the underparts, and is streaked in the male while being a uniform pale yellow in the female. The underparts of the female are paler than those of the male, and the female has a yellowish patch in front of the eyes. The beak is relatively stout and is black in both sexes.<ref name=ridgely>{{ cite book | last1=Ridgely | first1=Robert S. | last2=Tudor | first2=Guy | year=2009 | title=Birds of South America: Passerines | series=Helm Field Guides | location=London | publisher=Christopher Helm | isbn=978-1-408-11342-4 | page=615 }}</ref>

==Behaviour==
The yellow-lored tanager sometimes forms small groups of 3-4 individuals. It eats [[arthropods]] and fruit.<ref name=hbw>{{ cite book | last=Hilty | first=S.L. | year=2011 | chapter=Family Thraupidae (Tanagers) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=16: Tanagers to New World Blackbirds | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-96553-78-1 | pages=46-329 [321-322] | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0016unse/page/321/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref>

==Status==
The population of this bird has not been quantified but the total number of birds is thought to be declining. However, the bird has a very large range and the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] considers its conservation status to be of  "[[Least-concern species|least concern]]".<ref name=iucn/>
